coredata.dc:title: Arginine-functionalized graphene quantum dots@palladium composite modified pencil graphite electrode for electrochemical detection of DNA-mitomycin C interaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Denizhan, Nuray
Yilmaz, Selehattin
Ozkan-Ariksoysal, Dilsat
Emre, Deniz
Bilici, Ali
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, modified pencil graphite electrodes were prepared by synthesizing a novel nanocomposite with palladium and arginine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Arg@GQDs) for the electrochemical monitoring of anticancer compound mitomycin C (MC) and double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) interaction for the first time. The oxidation responses of both guanine in DNA and the MC drug were measured in the same potential scanning range, and the drug-DNA interaction was determined by the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. To improve the performance of the developed system, experimental parameters such as dsDNA and MC concentration and their interaction times were optimized. The surfaces obtained after the modification were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Ultraviolet-Visible Spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS), Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) methods. The developed nanocomposite-modified electrodes (Arg@GQD@Pd@PGEs) provided higher guanine oxidation signals than PGEs. The limit of detection (LOD) values for dsDNA immobilized on PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGEs were 0.713 pg 50 μL−1 and 0.019 pg 50 μL−1, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA drug interaction
Quantum dots
Biosensor
Palladium
Nanocomposite
Mitomycin C
Voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Arg@GQDs, Arg@GQDs@Pd, and Arg@GQDs@Pd@PGE
ds-DNA immobilization on bare and modified electrode surfaces
MC immobilization on bare and modified electrode surfaces
Material synthesis, electrode modification, and characterization
Characterization of electrodes modified by the electropolymerization technique
Electrochemical sensing of dsDNA using PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE
Voltammetric detection of the interaction between MC and dsDNA using Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was prepared from a part of the PhD dissertation of Nuray Denizhan. The authors thank Professor Ali Sinag (\u0130stanbul Ayd\u0131n University, T\u00FCrkiye) and Dr. Selva Bilge Yucel (Ankara University, Faculty of Science, Department of Chemistry, T\u00FCrkiye) for EIS measurements. The authors also thank Yucel Koc (MSc Chemical Eng. Eskisehir Osmangazi University, Faculty of Engineering and Architecture, Department of Chemical Engineering, T\u00FCrkiye) for interpreting the EIS equivalent circuit model, and Professor Deniz Yuzbasioglu (Gazi University, Faculty of Science, Department of Biology, T\u00FCrkiye) for supplying MC.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis scheme for preparing Arg@GQDs, Arg@GQDs@Pd, and Arg@GQDs@Pd@PGE modified electrodes.
FTIR spectra of Arg@GQDs and Arg@GQDs@Pd.

SEM images of Arg@GQDs (a) and Arg@GQD@Pd (b), Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE (c), and EDS mapping images of Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE (d-h): Carbon map (d), nitrogen map (e), oxygen map (f), palladium map (g) and overlapped map (h). EDX analyses of Arg@GQDs (i) and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE (j).

DPVs of guanine signals at bare PGE (a) and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE (b) in 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μg mL−1 dsDNA concentrations. Plots of the peak currents versus dsDNA concentrations (40, 60, 80, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500 μg mL−1) for bare PGE (c) and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE (d). The inset figures in (c) and (d) belong to the calibration plots in the linearity range. The guanine signals of PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE at 300 μg mL−1 DNA under optimum conditions are shown in the histogram (e). Measurements were made in ABS (0.2 M pH 4.80) buffer.

DPV voltammograms show the guanine oxidation responses at about (0.797 V) obtained from 300 μg mL−1 DNA (a), 300 μg mL−1 DNA + 200 μg mL−1 MC interaction (b), 200 μg mL−1 MC (c) on PGE (A), Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE (B), the corresponding peak current values shown in a histogram (C). The analyses were performed using 0.2 M ABS (pH 4.80) buffer.
Means of the EIS fitting results for the equivalent Randles circuit (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
R
s : Solution resistance, R
ct : Charge transfer resistance, Q: Constant phase element (CPE) coefficient, α: CPE exponent, Z
w : Warburg impedance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
ΔE
p[a-c] peak separations for PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE.
The effect of interfering agents on the peak current of dsDNA.
Comparison of the analytical performance of DNA/mitomycin C biosensors in the literature.

row.entry: Working electrode used in the DPV technique
Slope of the calibration line (sensitivity)/μA μg−1 mL−1
10 μg mL−1 fish sperm DNA- 5 μg mL−1 MC

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: GNF: Biocompatible hybrid nanoflowers (NFs) were synthesized by amino acids (glycine)

head.ce:title: Arginine-functionalized graphene quantum dots@palladium composite modified pencil graphite electrode for electrochemical detection of DNA-mitomycin C interaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Canakkale Onsekiz Mart University, Vocational School of Health Services, Department of Pharmacy Services, Canakkale, Türkiye
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Canakkale Onsekiz Mart University, Vocational School of Health Services, Department of Pharmacy Services, Canakkale, Türkiye

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, modified pencil graphite electrodes were prepared by synthesizing a novel nanocomposite with palladium and arginine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (Arg@GQDs) for the electrochemical monitoring of anticancer compound mitomycin C (MC) and double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) interaction for the first time. The oxidation responses of both guanine in DNA and the MC drug were measured in the same potential scanning range, and the drug-DNA interaction was determined by the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method. To improve the performance of the developed system, experimental parameters such as dsDNA and MC concentration and their interaction times were optimized. The surfaces obtained after the modification were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Ultraviolet-Visible Spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS), Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) methods. The developed nanocomposite-modified electrodes (Arg@GQD@Pd@PGEs) provided higher guanine oxidation signals than PGEs. The limit of detection (LOD) values for dsDNA immobilized on PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGEs were 0.713 pg 50 μL−1 and 0.019 pg 50 μL−1, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article and its supplementary information files.

ce:section.ce:para: Mitomycin C (MC) from Streptomyces caespitosus, discovered as an antibiotic in Japan in 1956, has been widely used as a therapeutic agent against tumors [1]. MC effectively treats colorectal, bladder, and gastric cancers, while also lowering the rates of tumor recurrence in cases deemed low to intermediate risk [2]. MC is a potent alkylating agent that cross-links DNA and inhibits DNA transcription to RNA, thereby preventing protein synthesis [2]. Therefore, the investigation of these interactions with various analytical methodologies, including infrared (IR), Raman, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis), electrophoresis, viscosity and thermal denaturation measurements, voltammetry, and atomic force microscopy (AFM) has excellent importance [3]. Among them, voltammetry is one of the most preferred techniques for the determination of the mentioned interactions due to its cheapness, simplicity, less time-consuming, and high sensitivity. In the voltametric techniques, electrochemical performance criteria such as sensitivity, selectivity, and stability depend on selecting proper electrodes. To improve the electrode performance, their surfaces are often modified with various nanoparticles such as graphene and its derivatives, carbon nanotubes, polymers, metals, and metal oxides [4].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are carbon materials composed of small graphene pieces with a diameter of approximately 10 nm [5]. GQDs have been used in electrochemical sensor applications in recent years due to their superior physicochemical properties, such as ease of synthesis, large surface area, various functional groups, sensitivity to chemical reactions, and non-toxic structure [6]. However, the application areas of GQDs can be further extended by functionalizing them with various materials, including metals, metal oxides, metal sulfides, polymers, and metal organic frameworks [6]. Recently, GQDs were especially functionalized with amino acids and noble metals. Amino acid functionalization enables the generation of a stable complex structure between GQDs and target analytes and enhances the voltammetric signal [7]. On the other hand, noble metals, including Pd, Au and Ru, present valuable characteristics that attract the attention of researchers, including large surface area, good stability, high conductivity and catalytic effect, lack of toxicity, and such a functionalization increases electron transfer rate and mass transport, bring about an enhanced analytical signal [6,8]. These kinds of hybrid modifiers were successfully used to investigate DNA-drug interactions. For example, the ds-DNA-dimethoate interaction mechanism was investigated on the graphene oxide (GO)/GQD nanocomposite modified GCE [9]. This sensor provided high selectivity, a rapid response, a low detection limit, and reproducibility [9]. Another study reported DNA-topotecan interaction on ionic liquid (IL), GQDs, and ds-DNA immobilized carbon paste electrode (CPE). Their system exhibited high stability with good sensitivity and selectivity [10]. In our previous study, we developed GQD@polyfluorene (PF) modified PGE to determine the voltammetric investigation of DNA and the anticancer drug MC interaction. Approximately 56 times higher response (enhanced sensitivity) was obtained with the GQD@PF modified PGE compared to the electrode without any modification [11].

In this study, initially, GQDs were functionalized with arginine (Arg) amino acid and Pd nanoparticles by considering their outstanding characteristics. Then, the prepared novel nanocomposite, Arg@GQDs@Pd, was used as a transducer modifier to determine the electrochemical interaction between dsDNA and MC. The characterizations of the synthesized materials and the modified electrode surfaces were performed in detail using HR-TEM, SEM, EDX, XRD, UV–Vis, FTIR, XPS, TGA, EIS, and CV methods. To explain the signal increase due to the use of modified PGE, a comprehensive evaluation was performed by comparing modified PGE with PGE that was not modified with any nanocomposite. The following sections analyze the study's findings regarding expected future results.

L-arginine (+99 %), citric acid (+99 %), and palladium (II) acetate (+98 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The dialysis membrane and PVDF filter (0.22 μm pore size) were purchased from Biotech and Aldrich, respectively.

Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), obtained from herring sperm, was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Onko Kocsel Pharmaceuticals kindly supplied MC. MC stock solution and dsDNA (1000 μg mL−1) were prepared in ultrapure water and stored in the refrigerator. Before the studies, this concentration was diluted to 500 μg mL−1 as the main stock, and the desired concentrations were prepared from this main stock. The chemicals used (citric acid 99 %; NaOH 99 %; NaCl 99 %, Acetic acid 99 %) were supplied from Sigma. Tombow (0.5 mm 2HB) PGEs were used in the studies.

A Bruker AXS model diffractometer, a Perkin-Elmer Win Lab 25 model UV–vis spectrophotometer, and a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum 100 model FTIR spectrometer were used for XRD, UV–vis, and FTIR analysis, respectively. For XRD studies, CuΚα radiation (k = 1.54 Å) was used. A FEI TALOS F200S TEM instrument (200 kV) and A Zeiss Sigma 300 model SEM instrument were exploited to conduct the TEM and SEM images of samples. A Metrohm 757 VA Trace Analyzer model potentiostat (Herisau, Switzerland) with a 10 mL voltametric cell, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a platinum auxiliary electrode was used for the electrochemical tests. Before analysis, argon gas was passed through the analyte solution for 60 s. DPV parameters were pulse amplitude 50 mV, pulse duration 0.04 s, voltage step 0.009 V, voltage step time 0.04, and potential step 10 mV. pH measurements were performed at 15 to 25 °C using an Adwa AD12 pH meter. Impedance measurements were carried out using Ivium CompactStat2.h potentiostat (frequencies of 0.01–300 kHz, under 5 mV constant AC voltage, 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) mixture, in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl. Thermogravimetric analysis was performed using a Perkin-Elmer TGA8000 instrument at 30–1000 °C (10 °C/min heating rate). XPS analyses were conducted using a Thermo-Scientific Κ-Alpha instrument equipped with a monochromatic Al Κα-ray source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Arg@GQDs, Arg@GQDs@Pd, and Arg@GQDs@Pd@PGE
ce:section.ce:para: The first step is based on the synthesis of Arg@GQDs [12]. Briefly, 2 g citric acid (CA) and 0.5 g L-Arginine (Arg) were mixed well in a 100 mL beaker and heated on a hot plate at 160 °C for approximately 20 min. During the heating, the color of the CA/Arg mixture changed from colorless to light yellow and then to orange. After adding 10 mL deionized water (DW), the pH of this solution was adjusted to 7.00 with 1 mol L−1 NaOH. Later, the solution was filtered (0.22 μm PVDF) and dialyzed using a dialysis membrane in a 500 mL beaker. DW was refreshed every 6 h during dialysis, which continued for a week. In the second step, 30 mg Pd(CH3COO)2 was dissolved in 50 mL water and then reduced with 5 mL NaBH4 solution (200 mg/10 mL DW). The resulting dispersion was centrifuged, and the precipitates were washed with DW three times and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, a mixture of 90 mg Arg@GQDs and 10 mg Pd nanoparticles was dispersed ultrasonically in 20 mL DW for 30 min. After evaporating the solvent, the resulting solid was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h, grounded, and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C [13].

The electrode surface was modified as described in the literature [11]. Arg@GQD@Pd (1000 μg mL−1) in ultrapure water was sonicated in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. The prepared nanocomposite was then diluted with PBS buffer (0.067 mol L−1, pH 7.00) to a concentration of 10 μg mL−1 and added to the electrochemical cell. PGE (Tombow 0.5 HB) was immersed in this cell by keeping its height constant and modified by taking voltammograms for 5 consecutive cycles at 100 mV s−1 in the potential range of −1.5 to +2.5 V using the CV technique. The voltammogram showing the successful modification of the PGE surface is shown in Supplementary Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ds-DNA immobilization on bare and modified electrode surfaces
ce:section.ce:para: Bare PGE and Arg@GQDs@Pd@PGE were placed in 200 μL plastic PCR tubes containing the specified concentration and 50 μL of ds-DNA solution prepared in ABS (0.2 mol L−1, pH: 4.80) for 20 min. After the ds-DNA was immobilized, the PGEs were rinsed twice with ABS to remove the biomolecule that was not immobilized on the surface. Voltammetric measurements were taken using blank ABS (0.2 mol L−1, pH: 4.80) and the DPV method. The calibration graphs obtained are shown in Fig. 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MC immobilization on bare and modified electrode surfaces
ce:section.ce:para: Bare PGE and Arg@GQDs@Pd@PGE were immersed in vials including 50 μL of MC solution at desired concentrations prepared in PBS (0.067 mol L−1, pH: 7.00) for 30 min. After MC immobilization on the PGE surface, the electrodes were washed by immersing in PBS twice to remove MC not attached to the surface. The DPV method was used in measurements performed in PBS (0.067 mol L−1, pH: 7.00) buffer.

PGEs were first immersed in 300 μg mL−1 ds-DNA solution (0.2 mol L−1 ABS pH 4.80) prepared in the vial and waited for 20 min. After immobilization, the electrode surface was rinsed twice with ABS to remove unbound DNA. Then, dsDNA-immobilized PGEs were immersed in an MC (200 μg mL−1) solution prepared in PBS (0.067 mol L−1, pH 7.00) to provide dsDNA-MC interaction for 30 min. After washing twice with PBS to remove excess unbound MC from the electrode surface, measurements with DPV were performed in ABS (pH 4.80) solution. The results are given in Fig. 8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material synthesis, electrode modification, and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The modified electrode was prepared in four steps. First, Arg@GQDs were prepared from the pyrolysis of the CA and Arg mixture [7,12]. Then, palladium nanoparticles (Pd NPs) were prepared by reducing Pd salt with NaBH4. The composite material was obtained by mixing Arg@GQDs and Pd dispersions ultrasonically. The last step involved electrochemically modifying the PGE surface with Arg@GQDs@Pd. The synthesis scheme is presented in Fig. 1 .

Arg@GQDs solution was light yellow under daylight (Supplementary Fig. S2a, inset 1) and exhibited a bright blue luminescence under 365 nm UV irradiation (Supplementary Fig. S2a, inset 2). The adsorption spectra of Arg@GQDs and Arg@GQDs@Pd are given in Supplementary Fig. S2a. Arg@GQDs displayed two adsorption maxima at 244 nm and 360 nm due to the presence of aromatic CC bonds (π → π* transitions) and oxygen-containing groups (n → π* transitions) in its structure [14,15]. Arg@GQDs@Pd exhibited a broader absorption band, and the absorption edge was red-shifted compared to Arg@GQDs. The optical band gaps of Arg@GQDs and Arg@GQDs@Pd were calculated from Tauc plots [16] and found to be 3.35 and 3.10 eV, respectively (Supplementary Fig. S2b). The integration of palladium nanoparticles into the Arg@GQDs structure increased the conductivity.

Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR spectrum of Arg@GQDs and Arg@GQDs@Pd. FT-IR spectrum of Arg@GQDs displayed -OH and NH stretching vibrations centered at 3454 cm−1, -CH stretching vibrations at 2941 cm−1, aromatic CC stretching vibrations at 1577 cm−1, and -OH bending vibrations at 1388 cm−1, and compatible with previous work [17]. Integrating Pd nanoparticles into Arg@GQDs changed the spectrum's peak positions and intensities. Due to composite formation, the 1350–650 cm−1 peaks were broadened, as seen in Fig. 2.

Fig. 3 presents the XRD diffractograms of Arg@GQDs and Arg@GQDs@Pd. Arg@GQDs exhibited only one broad peak, located at 20°. This peak can be associated with graphitic carbon's crystalline plane (002) [17]. Besides the Arg@GQDs diffraction peak (002), the composite structure's characteristic palladium peaks were observed. The peaks observed at 40.1, 46.6° and 68.1° were attributed to (111), (200), (220) planes of face-centered cubic palladium, respectively [18].

Fig. 4 shows the HRTEM images of the composite material. The images showed that nanoparticles in the 5–10 nm range and aggregates of 50–200 nm were formed (Fig. 4a, b). Fig. 4c demonstrated a clear lattice fringe with an interplanar spacing of 0.230 nm, which was attributed to (111) crystalline planes of fcc Pd [19]. However, identification of the GQD phase by TEM analysis was difficult, probably due to its poor crystallinity and low electron density, as reported before [19].

The thermal decomposition of the composite was analyzed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) over a temperature range of 30 to 1000 °C, and a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The TGA curve presented in Fig. S3 indicated that the thermal decomposition of Arg@GQD@Pd occurred in three main steps. The initial 8 % weight loss below 100 °C was likely due to the release of adsorbed solvent [20]. The primary decomposition (about 61 %) was in the range of 100–530 °C, which was attributed to the oxygen-containing functional groups located at the edges and basal planes of Arg@GQD [20]. The final decomposition (about 9 %) occurred between 750 and 890 °C, likely as a result of removing pyridine and pyrrolidine groups in Arg@GQD [21]. After reaching 890 °C, a plateau was observed in the thermogram, and about 20 % of the residue remained, probably due to the presence of palladium and carbon char.

A high-resolution XPS was performed to determine the nature of bonding among Pd, C, N, and O and the oxidation states in Arg@GQD@Pd (Fig. S4). The survey spectra exhibited peaks at 284.6, 399.2, 497.1, and 532.6 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of C 1 s, N 1 s, Na KL1, O 1 s, and Pd 3d, respectively (Fig. S4a). In the C 1 s spectrum, the peaks at 284.6, 286.1, and 288.1 eV were attributed to the presence of −C − C/−CH, −C − O/−CN, and − C=O/O − C=O functional groups, respectively [22,23] (Fig. S4b). The spectrum observed in Fig. S4c displayed peaks at 397.6 eV and 399.7 eV, which were assigned to the −C − N and − N − H functional groups, respectively [22]. The O 1 s spectrum observed in Fig. S4d presented peaks at 530.6, 531.7, and 535.3 eV, which corresponded to the HO–CO/Pd–O, OC functional groups, and ONa bonds (sodium residues, probably due to NaOH used in the neutralization process), respectively. XPS scans of Pd 3d revealed peaks at approximately 335.0 eV and 340.4 eV, corresponding to metallic palladium's 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 electrons, respectively (Fig. S4e) [19].

Fig. 5 shows SEM and EDS mapping images, and EDX profiles of Arg@GQDs, Arg@GQDs@Pd, and Arg@GQDs@Pd@PGE surfaces. The surface of Arg@GQDs was almost flat and contained cracks at low magnifications (Fig. S5a). However, spherical and adherent particle formations were observed at higher magnifications, and their sizes were in the range of 20–50 nm, as seen in Fig. 5a. The composite had a heterogenous morphology (Fig. 5b). However, images at higher magnification exhibited the presence of spherical and partly agglomerated particles. Their sizes were in the range of 10–200 nm (Supplementary Fig. S5b). The modified electrode had coarse grains, cracks, and a rough surface (Fig. 5c). As seen in Supplementary Fig. S5c, it was obvious that the surface of the particles was covered with a film layer. The bright and dark parts observed in Fig. 5c were assigned to metallic and nonmetallic phases, respectively. As the beam was focused on bright areas, the palladium peak intensity increased. However, its intensity was highly reduced as the darker regions were focused (Fig. 5j). Fig. 5j also indicated that the coexistence of signals belonging to composite components, Arg@GQDs (C, N, O, and Na) (Fig. 5i), palladium nanoparticles, and PGE (1.70 eV for Si) [24,25]. EDS mapping analyses given in Fig. 5d-h also showed both the presence and distribution of the elements on the PGE surface. These data confirmed successful electrode modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of electrodes modified by the electropolymerization technique
ce:section.ce:para: The modification process of the Arg@GQD@Pd nanocomposite was performed on the PGE surface by performing five successive CV scans (100 mV s−1 scan rate, −1.5 V to +2.5 V potential). The voltammetric and impedimetric measurement results of Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE obtained with this modification are presented in Supplementary Fig. S6 and compared with the bare PGE results.
Fig. S6a shows Nyquist plots with best-fit equivalent circuit models. Table 1 presents the results of the EIS fitting derived from the equivalent circuit illustrated in Fig. S6a.

The average Rct values of bare and modified PGEs were measured to be 1.94 × 102 Ω and 3.79 × 10−3 Ω, respectively. After the PGE surface was modified with Arg@GQD@Pd nanocomposite, the Rct value was calculated to decrease by 99.99 % for Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE. This difference in response can be interpreted as faster electron transfer between the electrode interface (Nernst diffusion layer) and the solution due to the increased surface area with the Arg@GQD@Pd modification [26,27]. The decrease in charge transfer resistance by modification increases the electroactive surface area and conductivity, promoting the binding of the analyte to the electrode surface, enhancing the electron transfer, and increasing the peak current [27,28]. In the equivalent circuit model, the CPE simulates the electric field between the electrode surface and the surrounding electrolyte, functioning as an imperfect capacitor. A higher electrode capacitance corresponds to a greater charge storage capacity [28,29]. After the modifications, the CPE coefficient Q increased, indicating improved charge storage capability. The CPE exponent α quantitatively characterizes both the roughness of the surface morphology of the electrodes and the uniformity of current distribution. Typically, α ranges from 0.5 to 1 under standard electrochemical conditions, with a value close to 1 indicating an ideally smooth surface and uniform current flow. In contrast, lower values signify increased surface heterogeneity and non-uniform charge transfer dynamics. The α CPE exponent value of the modified electrode is closer to 1 than that of the bare electrode, indicating that the electrode surface is smoother and the electron distribution on the surface is more homogeneous [29].

The CV method was used to investigate the electrochemical characteristics of Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE and bare PGE. The measurements made with PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE were carried out with K3[Fe(CN)6] (1 mmol L−1) solution containing KCl (100 mmol L−1) in the electrochemical cell (Fig. S6b). The average i p value of bare PGE was found to be 2.65 ± 0.1 μA with the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.8 % (n = 3) obtained at K3[Fe(CN)6] (1 mmol L−1) containing KCl (100 mmol L−1) (Fig. S6a). After the modification of Arg@GQD@Pd onto the PGE surface, a significant increase in the anodic peak current was obtained. The average value of the i p obtained from modified PGE was measured as 22.7 ± 1 μA and its RSD was found to be 4.4 % (n = 3) (Supplementary Fig. S6b). The results show that Arg@GQD@Pd significantly enlarges the electroactive surface area, improving the observed analytical signal.
In addition, the peak separations (ΔEp[a-c]) of oxidation-reduction pairs for PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE were also evaluated. These results are presented in Table 2 .

The difference between the redox couples (ΔEp [a-c]) in bare PGE was 87 mV, while it increased to 101 mV in Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE. This is evidence that the electrode surface properties and electroactivity changed after the Arg@GQD@Pd modification [30,31].

The electroactive surface area (ESA) was also calculated for both unmodified PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd-PGE by the Randles-Ševčík Eq. [32] According to the results of Supplementary Fig. S6b, the ESA of PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd-PGE were found to be 0.0036 (±0.0002) cm2 and 1.223 (±0.008) cm2, respectively. As a result, after the electrode modification with Arg@GQD@Pd, an approximately 36-fold increase in ESA was detected compared to before the modification.

The stability and long-term performance of Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE electrodes were investigated. For this purpose, Arg@GQD@Pd modified electrodes were kept in supporting electrolyte for 1 day, and no significant difference was observed in the current values obtained from the same sample. However, after 1 day, the current value decreased by more than 70 %. Arg@GQD@Pd modified electrodes were stable for 1 day in the supporting electrolyte. Therefore, modified electrodes should be prepared and used daily.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing of dsDNA using PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE
ce:section.ce:para: The detection performances of bare and nanocomposite-modified (Arg@GQD@Pd) PGE surfaces were compared based on the magnitude of the guanine signal obtained from DPVs of dsDNA immobilized on these electrodes (Fig. 6a and b).

dsDNA at different concentrations ranging from 100 to 500 μg mL−1, prepared by diluting from the 500 μg mL−1 main stock, was immobilized on the sensor surfaces for 20 min. A gradual increment at the guanine signal was observed up to 300 μg mL−1 dsDNA immobilization from Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE. After this concentration value, although an increase in the signal obtained from the nanomaterial-modified sensor was observed, the reproducibility of the response decreased for the bare PGE (Fig. 6c and d).

The optimum dsDNA concentration value for immobilizing dsDNA on the Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE was selected as 300 μg mL−1 with good repeatability and reproducibility. 300 μg mL-1 dsDNA was immobilized onto the bare PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE surface to study the repeatability over multiple runs. Then, the average guanine responses were obtained as 0.49 ± 0.1 μA (% RSD (n = 3); 0.08 %) for bare PGE, and 15.5 ± 0.2 μA (% RSD (n = 3); 42 %) for the modified electrode (Fig. 6e).

According to the literature [33] and the IUPAC definition [34], the limit of detection (LOD) values were calculated as 0.713 pg/ 50 μL and 0.0192 pg/ 50 μL for PGE and Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE in a 50 μL sample volume, respectively, using the linear section of the calibration line in Fig. 6c and d (Eq. 1):
(1)
LOD = 3
S b
/ q

In Eq. 1, S
b is the blank solution's standard deviation (the supporting electrolyte in the absence of dsDNA is used as the blank solution), and q is the slope of the calibration plot (Fig. 6c and d insets).

The guanine oxidation signals measured with Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE showed an increase of approximately 32-fold compared to bare PGE (Fig. 6e). Fig. 6c and 8d inset calibration plots were selected in the linearity range. The R2 value is closer to 1 in the modified electrode (0.9976) than in the bare one (0.9769). This shows that the nanocomposite modified PGE has a higher power in explaining the changes in the y-axis (peak current values) against increasing DNA concentration than the bare electrode. In other words, the sensitivity of the modified electrode is higher than the bare sensor, which means that the linearity of the relevant calibration line is also better than the bare one (Fig. 6c and d). When the increases in the signals obtained by dsDNA immobilization on bare and Arg@GQD@Pd nanocomposite-modified surfaces at increasing concentrations are compared, there is a more increase in Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE than in bare PGE, indicating that the relevant modification increases the surface area. For bare PGE, a comparison is presented for both electrodes starting from the concentration value at which the peak appearance becomes visible (Fig. 7a, b). In addition, these nanometer-sized materials have large surface areas and high surface free energy, so they can strongly adsorb many biomolecules on their surfaces. This puts them in a unique position in biosensor designs [35]. On the other hand, arginine is positively charged at physiological pH due to its guanidinium group. This charge may exert electrostatic attraction on DNA's negatively charged phosphate backbone, allowing more DNA to bind to the surface. The combination of these factors enhanced DNA binding to the surface [36–38]. Palladium also improves conductivity and signal (Fig. S7) [39].

When we compare the LOD of the electrodes, the LOD obtained for Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE was lower than that of PGE (Fig. 6d, e). In line with these findings, it was shown that the electrode surface modified with Arg@GQD@Pd nanocomposite developed in the study detected dsDNA more sensitively than the bare PGE surface. As a result, it was decided to work with the Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE surface in subsequent experiments to investigate the drug-DNA interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric detection of the interaction between MC and dsDNA using Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE
ce:section.ce:para: The main objective of this research is to develop an alternative electrochemical method for determining the interaction between MC and dsDNA. For this aim, MC was prepared in the 100–500 μg mL−1 concentration range and immobilized on the Arg@GQD@Pd modified PGE for 30 min to examine its behavior on the electrode surface. The oxidation peaks of guanine and MC in dsDNA were presented in voltammograms because they could be obtained in the same voltammetric potential range for bare and modified PGE (Fig. 7A and B).

According to the DPV measurements, the signal of guanine was measured as 8.464 μA using Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE before the MC-dsDNA interaction (Fig. 7B). This guanine signal was measured as 5.195 μA after the MC-dsDNA interaction, which means that there was a 38.63 % decrease in the guanine peak current after the interaction (Fig. 7C).

The signals related to this interaction became much more pronounced with the nanomaterial modification than the bare electrode (Fig. 7B). After DNA-MC interaction, the guanine signal obtained from the bare electrode was approximately 0.12 μA, while the signal obtained from the modified electrode under the same conditions was approximately 5.195 μA. The difference between these signals was almost 43-fold, proving the nanomaterial modification's enhancing effect on the signal. To summarize, the use of nanomaterials had an enriching impact on the interaction signal. On the other hand, the difference rate between the signals obtained before and after the interaction decreased by nearly 90 % in the bare electrode, while it was 40 % in the modified electrode. Although this situation caused the difference rate with the bare electrode to seem higher and more advantageous than the modified one, the fact that the signals obtained with the bare electrode were low and poorly defined was a disadvantage affecting accuracy and precision.

The decrease in this signal resulting from the oxidation of the guanine base in DNA can be explained by the affinity of MC to GC pairs and thus binding to double-stranded DNA [40–42]. The decline in the guanine response is also found in literature investigating the results of dsDNA and MC interaction [12,43–45]. According to the literature, MC, which damages DNA in healthy and cancerous cells and has a cytotoxic character, interacts with the guanine base and makes cross-links. As a result, it disrupts DNA synthesis [33,34,46–48]. Thus, it inhibits mitosis by preventing DNA replication. [32,40,48]. This study, which is consistent with previous literature and supports earlier studies with new findings, also showed that the anticancer drug MC reduces the magnitude of the guanine oxidation response.

Selectivity studies were conducted. The analytical signal change in the absence and presence of ascorbic acid, citric acid, and uric acid was examined to demonstrate the interference effect. For this aim, 300 μg mL−1 DNA immobilized Arg@Pd@PGE interacted with 200 μg mL−1 MC in the presence of 200 μg mL−1 interferents. The bias values for peak currents were less than 2 %. These results indicated almost no interference effect (Table 3 and Fig. S8).

The analytical parameters of the presented sensors were compared with previously reported voltammetric DNA/mitomycin C biosensors (Table 4 ). These data indicate that prepared modified PGEs perform satisfactorily.

In this work, a novel high-sensitivity electrochemical nanobiosensor using a single-use carbon-based graphite electrode modified with Arg@GQD@Pd was designed, characterized, and evaluated for dsDNA sensing. The detection limit of the nanobiosensor was 0.019 pg/ 50 μL. The interaction between dsDNA and MC was also studied. Before measuring the interaction of dsDNA with MC, the Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE surface was used to immobilize dsDNA and MC separately. This study aimed to determine whether the binding of dsDNA biomolecules and MC drug molecules showed increased binding to the electrode surface. As a result of the experiments, it was recorded that the binding of dsDNA to the Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE surface was increased by 30.63-fold compared to PGE. The binding of MC to the Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE surface was increased 6-fold compared to PGE. It was recorded that the guanine peak signal measured with bare PGE was 0.49 μA, and the guanine peak signal measured with Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE was 15.5 μA. This result proved that Arg@GQD@Pd@PGE provided more sensitive results than PGE. The nanocomposite-based electrochemical method developed in this study has brought a new alternative to those presented in previous studies. It has some advantages, such as easy use, affordable cost, and disposable use. These features are essential for the biosensor R&D field. Moreover, the inexpensive and easily synthesized Arg@GQD@Pd showed high performance in the dsDNA detection. It is evaluated that Arg@GQD@Pd nanocomposite modified disposable electrodes can be used in DNA detection studies, DNA-drug interaction studies, and the development of new molecules for the cancer field.

Nuray Denizhan: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Selehattin Yilmaz: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Dilsat Ozkan-Ariksoysal: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Deniz Emre: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ali Bilici: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This study was prepared from a part of the PhD dissertation of Nuray Denizhan. The authors thank Professor Ali Sinag (İstanbul Aydın University, Türkiye) and Dr. Selva Bilge Yucel (Ankara University, Faculty of Science, Department of Chemistry, Türkiye) for EIS measurements. The authors also thank Yucel Koc (MSc Chemical Eng., Eskisehir Osmangazi University, Faculty of Engineering and Architecture, Department of Chemical Engineering, Türkiye) for interpreting the EIS equivalent circuit model, and Professor Deniz Yuzbasioglu (Gazi University, Faculty of Science, Department of Biology, Türkiye) for supplying MC.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2025.112433.

A.J. Bard, L.R. Faulkner, H.S. White, Electrochemical methods: fundamentals and applications, 3rd Edition, Wiley, New York, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical genosensor for Mitomycin C–DNA interaction based on guanine signal

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interactions of surface-confined DNA with acid-activated mitomycin C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Verweij, H.M. Pinedo, Mitomycin C: mechanism of action, usefulness and limitations., Anticancer Drugs 1 (1990) 5–13.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene oxide integrated sensor for electrochemical monitoring of mitomycin C–DNA interaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Erdem, M. Muti, P. Papakonstantinou, E. Canavar, H. Karadeniz, G. Congur, S. Sharma, Graphene oxide integrated sensor for electrochemical monitoring of mitomycin C–DNA interaction, Analyst 137 (2012) 2129. doi:10.1039/c2an16011k.
